subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: What DFT is, why it matters, and where it fits in the chip design flow.
keyPoints: DFT = Design for Testability — making chips testable after manufacturing
Without DFT, you cannot know if a fabricated chip works correctly
Manufacturing defects: open circuits, short circuits, stuck-at faults
Stuck-at-0: a wire is always 0 regardless of logic; Stuck-at-1: always 1
Test coverage: % of all possible faults that can be detected
Industry target: 99%+ fault coverage before tapeout
DFT adds ~10-15% area overhead but is absolutely mandatory
subLevels.content: After a chip is manufactured, it must be tested on Automated Test Equipment (ATE) to confirm it works. Without DFT, internal nodes of the chip are inaccessible — you can only observe outputs. DFT adds structures (scan chains, BIST, JTAG) that make internal flip-flops observable and controllable from external pins. The most common defect model is stuck-at: a wire is permanently stuck at logic 0 or 1 due to a manufacturing defect. Test patterns are vectors applied at the chip inputs that propagate the effect of a fault to an observable output. Fault coverage measures what percentage of all possible stuck-at faults are detected by the test patterns.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Basic DFT RTL attributes — scan enable, test mode, and DFT ports.
keyPoints: scan_en: global scan enable signal — controls scan vs functional mode
test_mode: puts chip in test mode (disables clock gating etc.)
In scan mode: flip-flops form a shift register chain
In functional mode: flip-flops operate normally
SE (Scan Enable) high → scan shift mode
SE low → functional/capture mode
TDI/TDO: test data in/out — serial scan interface

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace through a 4-flop scan chain — how shift and capture works.
keyPoints: Scan chain: flip-flops connected SO→SI in a serial chain
Shift phase: scan_en=1, clock N times to shift in test pattern
Capture phase: scan_en=0, clock once to capture response
Shift-out phase: scan_en=1, clock N times to read out response
Compare: expected vs actual shift-out data = pass/fail
One scan chain of N flops needs N shift cycles

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a scan-capable D flip-flop with scan MUX.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 0: Scan-Capable Flip-Flop

Implement a scan-capable flip-flop:
```
module scan_dff (
  input  logic clk, rst_n,
  input  logic d,   // functional data
  input  logic si,  // scan data in
  input  logic se,  // scan enable
  output logic q,
  output logic so   // scan data out
);
```

Requirements:
1. When `se=1`: flop captures `si` (scan shift mode)
2. When `se=0`: flop captures `d` (functional mode)
3. `so` is always connected to `q` (scan out)
4. Active-low async reset: `rst_n=0` → `q=0`
5. Write a testbench that:
   - Shifts in pattern `1011` through scan chain
   - Verifies `so` matches expected after each clock
hints: logic d_mux; assign d_mux = se ? si : d;
always_ff @(posedge clk or negedge rst_n) if (!rst_n) q <= 0; else q <= d_mux;
assign so = q;  // scan out always equals Q
lab.solutionExplanation: A scan flop adds a 2:1 MUX before the D input. SE=1 selects SI (scan input) for shifting test patterns. SE=0 selects D (functional data) for normal operation. SO=Q makes the flop's state observable by chaining SO to the next flop's SI.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your DFT fundamentals.

quiz.q: What does a stuck-at-1 fault mean?
options: A net is permanently logic 1 regardless of what drives it

quiz.q: Scan enable (SE=1) puts the chip in:
options: Scan shift mode — flops form a shift register
99% of all stuck-at faults can be detected

quiz.q: In a scan chain, SO of one flop connects to:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Scan chain insertion — connecting all flip-flops into testable chains.
keyPoints: Scan insertion replaces standard flops with scan flops automatically
All flip-flops in a design are chained together: FF0→FF1→FF2...→FFn
Multiple chains: long designs split into parallel chains for speed
Chain length: number of flops per chain — affects test time
TDI/TDO: test data input/output pins for each chain
Scan compression: reduces test time by compressing multiple chains
EDA tools: Synopsys DFT Compiler, Cadence Encounter DFT
subLevels.content: Scan insertion is performed automatically by EDA tools after synthesis. The tool replaces every standard flip-flop (DFF) with a scan flip-flop (SDFF) that has an extra MUX and scan port. All SDFFs are then connected into one or more chains: the SO of flop N connects to the SI of flop N+1. The first chain's SI is connected to the TDI pin; the last chain's SO is TDO. Multiple parallel chains reduce test time — instead of shifting N bits serially, you shift N/M bits in M parallel chains. Chain balancing ensures all chains have roughly equal length for parallel efficiency.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: RTL coding guidelines for scan-friendly design.
keyPoints: Avoid combinational loops — cause hold violations in scan mode
Avoid gated clocks without scan bypass — blocks scan shifting
Clock gating cell: add SE to bypass gate when scanning
Tristate buses: disable during scan (use scan_en to force enable)
Async sets/resets: must be controllable from test pins
Avoid mixed-clock flops in same chain — causes timing issues
Reset all flops to known state before scan shift begins

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read a scan chain connection for a 3-flop design.
keyPoints: Original design: 3 independent DFFs
After scan insertion: 3 SDFFs chained
TDI → FF0.SI → FF1.SI → FF2.SI → TDO
SE controls all flops simultaneously
Test time = chain length × (shift + capture cycles)
Multiple chains run in parallel to reduce test time

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Build a 4-flop scan chain and verify shift/capture operation.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 1: 4-Flop Scan Chain

Build a module with 4 scan flip-flops chained together:

```
module scan_chain_4 (
  input  logic clk, rst_n,
  input  logic d0, d1, d2, d3,  // functional data
  input  logic scan_en,
  input  logic tdi,
  output logic tdo,
  output logic q0, q1, q2, q3
);
```

Chain: tdi → FF0 → FF1 → FF2 → FF3 → tdo

Testbench must:
1. Reset all flops
2. Shift in pattern `1101` (MSB first through TDI)
3. Check q3,q2,q1,q0 = 1,1,0,1 after 4 shift clocks
4. Capture mode (se=0): apply d0=1,d1=0,d2=1,d3=1, clock once
5. Shift out and verify TDO sequence
hints: scan_dff ff0(.clk,.rst_n,.d(d0),.si(tdi),  .se(scan_en),.q(q0),.so(so0));
scan_dff ff1(.clk,.rst_n,.d(d1),.si(so0), .se(scan_en),.q(q1),.so(so1));
scan_dff ff2(.clk,.rst_n,.d(d2),.si(so1), .se(scan_en),.q(q2),.so(so2));
scan_dff ff3(.clk,.rst_n,.d(d3),.si(so2), .se(scan_en),.q(q3),.so(tdo));
lab.solutionExplanation: Each scan flop's SO connects to the next flop's SI, creating a serial chain. TDI enters FF0, and FF3's SO is TDO. During shift (SE=1), data moves one position per clock. After 4 clocks, the 4-bit pattern is loaded. Capture (SE=0) freezes functional data into the chain.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your scan chain knowledge.

quiz.q: Why are multiple scan chains used instead of one long chain?
options: To reduce test time by shifting in parallel

quiz.q: Clock gating cells need scan bypass because:
options: Without TE, the gated clock blocks scan shifting

quiz.q: After shift-in, the capture phase uses SE=:
Scan chain length of N means:
options: N flip-flops in that chain — requires N shift clocks

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Fault models — stuck-at, transition, path delay, and bridging faults.
keyPoints: Stuck-at-0 (SA0): net permanently 0 regardless of logic driving it
Stuck-at-1 (SA1): net permanently 1 regardless of logic driving it
Transition fault: gate output cannot make a 0→1 or 1→0 transition
Path delay fault: critical path has additional delay due to defect
Bridging fault: two adjacent nets are shorted together
IDDQ testing: detects bridging by measuring quiescent current
Stuck-at is most common — 80%+ of DFT targets this model
subLevels.content: Different manufacturing defects manifest as different fault models. Stuck-at is the classic model — a physical defect (particle contamination, metal void) causes a net to be permanently driven to 0 or 1. Transition faults model slow circuits — a gate can toggle but too slowly, failing at-speed tests. Path delay faults are the hardest to test — they require launching and capturing at full functional speed. Bridging faults occur when two adjacent wires touch (short circuit) — they create a wired-AND or wired-OR depending on the drivers. Cell-aware faults model defects within a standard cell (like a floating gate).

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Writing testbench patterns to detect specific stuck-at faults.
keyPoints: To detect SA0 on net N: force N to logic 1, observe at output
To detect SA1 on net N: force N to logic 0, observe at output
Sensitization: set all other inputs so fault effect propagates to output
D-algorithm: classical ATPG method for finding test vectors
Test vector: {input values} that make a fault visible at output
Fault simulation: simulates which faults each vector detects
Untestable fault: fault that cannot be detected by any vector

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Find test vectors for all stuck-at faults on a 2-input OR gate.
keyPoints: OR gate: Y = A | B
SA0 on Y: need A=1 or B=1 → Y should be 1 but stuck=0
SA1 on Y: need A=0, B=0 → Y should be 0 but stuck=1
SA0 on A: need A=1, B=0 → effect propagates through B=0
SA1 on A: need A=0, B=0 → Y=0 fault-free, Y=1 faulty
Total faults on 2-input OR: 6 (SA0/SA1 on A, B, Y)
Some vectors detect multiple faults simultaneously

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Write test vectors to detect stuck-at faults on a combinational circuit.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 2: Fault Detection Test Vectors

Given a 2-level combinational circuit:
```
// G1: w1 = A & B  (AND)
// G2: w2 = B | C  (OR)
// G3: Y  = w1 ^ w2 (XOR)
```

Write a testbench that:
1. Implements the fault-free circuit
2. Tests these 4 specific stuck-at faults by applying the correct vector:
   - SA0 on w1: need w1=1 → A=1,B=1
   - SA1 on w1: need w1=0 → A=0 or B=0
   - SA0 on Y: find a vector where Y=1 fault-free
   - SA1 on w2: need w2=0 → B=0,C=0
3. For each test, display the inputs, expected output, and whether fault is detectable

Module name: `tb_fault_vectors`
hints: To detect SA0 on net N: the fault-free value of N must be 1
To detect SA1 on net N: the fault-free value of N must be 0
The effect must propagate to Y — all other paths must not mask it
apply_and_check(1,1,0, 1, "w1_SA0") tests A=1,B=1,C=0 expecting Y=1
lab.solutionExplanation: Detecting SA0 requires the fault-free value to be 1 (so the stuck-at-0 creates a 0→observable difference). Detecting SA1 requires fault-free=0. The effect must propagate through all remaining logic to reach Y — this is called sensitization. Each test vector can detect multiple faults simultaneously.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your fault model knowledge.

quiz.q: To detect SA0 on a net, the test vector must make that net:
options: Logic 1 (opposite of fault) so the difference is observable
Circuits that can toggle but too slowly at speed
Two flip-flops have the same data
Two adjacent wires are shorted together
Clock and data arrive at same time
A gate has too many fanouts

quiz.q: The most commonly targeted fault model in DFT is:

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: ATPG — Automatic Test Pattern Generation for maximum fault coverage.
keyPoints: ATPG automatically generates test vectors to detect stuck-at faults
D-algorithm: classic ATPG algorithm — places D (fault value) at fault site
PODEM: Path-Oriented Decision Making — more efficient than D-algorithm
Fault simulation: determines which faults each pattern detects
Redundant faults: logically impossible to detect — circuit structure issue
Tools: Synopsys TetraMAX, Cadence Encounter Test
Output: .stil or .wgl test pattern files for ATE
subLevels.content: ATPG tools automatically find input vectors that detect each fault in the design. The tool uses algorithms like PODEM or Fulls to: (1) activate the fault (set the net to opposite of stuck value), (2) propagate the fault effect to an observable output (scan chain or primary output), and (3) justify all required signal values backward through the circuit. Fault simulation then runs all generated patterns against all faults to compute coverage. Patterns are then optimized (compacted) to remove redundant vectors. The final output is a test program loaded onto ATE that tests actual silicon.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: ATPG concepts — D-notation, fault activation, and propagation.
keyPoints: D notation: D=1 fault-free, 0 faulty; D̄=0 fault-free, 1 faulty
Fault activation: set fault site to opposite of stuck value
Fault propagation: route D through sensitized path to output
Justification: backward implication to set required values
X (don't-care): input not needed — ATPG sets to reduce conflicts
Test cube: partial assignment {A=1, B=X, C=0} before full specification
Compaction: merge patterns with compatible X values to reduce count

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Run ATPG step by step on a small 3-gate circuit.
keyPoints: Circuit: Y = (A & B) | C
Target fault: SA0 on node w1 (A & B output)
Step 1: Activate w1=1 → set A=1, B=1
Step 2: Propagate w1=D through OR gate → set C=0
Step 3: Y=D (fault-free=1, faulty=0) — observable!
Final test vector: A=1, B=1, C=0, expect Y=1
Fault simulation confirms: SA0 on w1 detected by this vector

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement manual ATPG — find and verify test vectors for a circuit.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 3: Manual ATPG

For the circuit: `Y = (A XNOR B) & (B | C)`

Decomposed:
```
w1 = A ~^ B  (XNOR)
w2 = B | C   (OR)
Y  = w1 & w2 (AND)
```

Write a testbench that:
1. Implements the circuit
2. Finds and applies test vectors for these 6 faults:
   - SA0 on w1, SA1 on w1
   - SA0 on w2, SA1 on w2  
   - SA0 on Y,  SA1 on Y
3. For each fault, apply the correct detection vector
4. Print: which vector detects which fault
5. Count total faults detected

Module name: `tb_atpg_manual`
hints: XNOR: output=1 when both inputs equal; output=0 when inputs differ
w1 SA0 detection: need w1=1 in fault-free → A=B (e.g., A=1,B=1)
w1 SA1 detection: need w1=0 in fault-free → A≠B (e.g., A=0,B=1)
For AND output Y=1: both inputs must be 1
lab.solutionExplanation: Manual ATPG applies the D-algorithm principles: activate (set fault site to opposite value), propagate (route D to observable output), justify (set all required inputs). Notice how some vectors detect multiple faults — this is fault collapsing, which makes ATPG efficient.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your ATPG knowledge.

quiz.q: A redundant fault in ATPG is:
options: A fault logically impossible to detect — the circuit is overdesigned
A fault caught by multiple vectors
Which faults each test pattern detects
The speed of the ATPG algorithm
Standard Test Interface Language — format for ATE test programs

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: MBIST — Memory Built-In Self-Test for SRAMs and ROMs.
keyPoints: MBIST tests embedded memories without external ATE memory patterns
Memories cannot be tested by scan chains — need special algorithm
March algorithm: writes/reads patterns in specific sequences
March C-: most popular, detects stuck-at and coupling faults
Fault models: stuck-at, transition, coupling (two cells interact)
MBIST controller: on-chip FSM that runs march tests automatically
Pass/Fail signal: single output tells ATE if memory is good
subLevels.content: Embedded SRAMs and ROMs make up 60-70% of modern SoC area. They cannot be tested by conventional scan ATPG because their internal structure is a regular array, not random logic. MBIST inserts a controller that runs march algorithms — sequences of write and read operations that detect all stuck-at and coupling faults. The March C- algorithm: (↕W0)(↑R0W1)(↑R1W0)(↓R0W1)(↓R1W0)(↕R0). Each element means: go up/down the address space, write/read specific values. A BIST controller is a small FSM that generates these sequences and compares read data to expected — all on-chip, no ATE memory patterns needed.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: March algorithm patterns and MBIST controller state machine.
keyPoints: M0: ↕W0 — write 0 to all addresses (up or down order)
M1: ↑R0W1 — read 0 then write 1, ascending addresses
M2: ↑R1W0 — read 1 then write 0, ascending addresses
M3: ↓R0W1 — read 0 then write 1, descending addresses
M4: ↓R1W0 — read 1 then write 0, descending addresses
M5: ↕R0 — read 0 from all addresses
Total operations: 6N reads/writes for N-cell memory

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace through March C- algorithm on a 4-cell memory.
keyPoints: 4-cell memory: addr 0,1,2,3
M0: Write 0 to all → [0,0,0,0]
M1 ascending: R0,W1 at each addr → [1,1,1,1]
M2 ascending: R1,W0 at each addr → [0,0,0,0]
M3 descending: R0,W1 → [1,1,1,1]
M4 descending: R1,W0 → [0,0,0,0]
M5: Read all, expect 0 — any mismatch = fail

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a simple March-C MBIST controller for a small memory.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 4: MBIST Controller

Implement a simplified MBIST controller for an 8-cell memory:

```
module mbist_simple #(parameter DEPTH=8, AW=3) (
  input  logic clk, rst_n,
  input  logic bist_en,
  output logic bist_done,
  output logic bist_pass,
  output logic [AW-1:0] mem_addr,
  output logic          mem_we,
  output logic          mem_wdata,
  input  logic          mem_rdata
);
```

Implement 3-element simplified march:
1. **M0**: Write 0 to addresses 0..7 (ascending)
2. **M1**: Read (expect 0), Write 1 to addresses 0..7 (ascending) — fail if read≠0
3. **M2**: Read (expect 1) from addresses 0..7 — fail if read≠1
4. Set `bist_done=1` and `bist_pass=1` if no failures, `bist_pass=0` if any failure
hints: The state machine cycles: IDLE→M0_WRITE0→M1_RD0_WR1→M2_READ1→IDLE
In M1: read mem_rdata, if not 0 set fail=1, then write 1
In M2: read mem_rdata, if not 1 set fail=1
bist_pass = ~fail at the end of M2
lab.solutionExplanation: The MBIST controller is a simple FSM that sequences through march elements. Each element writes a known pattern and reads back to verify. Any mismatch sets a fail flag. The pass/fail output is readable by ATE with just one pin — much simpler than providing full memory test patterns externally.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your MBIST knowledge.

quiz.q: Why can't scan chains test embedded SRAMs?
options: SRAM internal array is not made of standard scan flip-flops
Stuck-at and coupling faults between memory cells
Pass or fail — single bit indicating memory health
Read expecting 0 then write 1, ascending addresses

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: LBIST — Logic Built-In Self-Test using LFSR and MISR.
keyPoints: LBIST tests combinational logic on-chip without ATE patterns
LFSR: Linear Feedback Shift Register — generates pseudo-random patterns
MISR: Multiple-Input Signature Register — compresses responses
Signature: compressed output of MISR after N patterns
Compare signature to golden reference → pass or fail
LBIST is slower than scan ATPG but works in the field
Used for periodic self-test in safety-critical chips (automotive)
subLevels.content: LBIST uses two key components: an LFSR as a pseudo-random pattern generator (PRPG) and a MISR as a signature analyzer. The LFSR automatically generates a sequence of pseudo-random test patterns without external ATE memory. These patterns are applied to the circuit under test (CUT) through the scan chain. Circuit responses are compressed by the MISR into a signature — a short fingerprint of all responses. After N patterns, the signature is compared to a pre-computed golden signature. A match means pass; mismatch means a defect was detected. LBIST is ideal for field testing (automotive, aerospace) where chips must self-test periodically during operation.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: LFSR and MISR implementation patterns in SystemVerilog.
keyPoints: LFSR: XOR feedback taps defined by primitive polynomial
4-bit LFSR: x^4 + x^3 + 1 → taps at bits 4 and 3
LFSR generates 2^N - 1 unique states (maximal length)
MISR: like LFSR but with additional XOR inputs from CUT
Aliasing: different faults producing the same signature (rare)
Aliasing probability: ~2^(-N) for N-bit MISR
Seed: initial LFSR value determines pattern sequence

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace 4 LFSR patterns and compute the MISR signature.
keyPoints: LFSR seed = 4'b1001
Pattern 1: 1001 → Pattern 2: 0011 → Pattern 3: 0110 → Pattern 4: 1101
Each pattern applied to CUT, response captured
MISR XORs each response into running signature
Final signature compared to golden value
Fault changes even one response → different signature → detected

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a 4-bit LFSR and verify the pseudo-random sequence.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 5: LFSR Pattern Generator

Implement a 4-bit maximal-length LFSR:
- Polynomial: x^4 + x^3 + 1 (taps at bits 3 and 2, 0-indexed)
- Feedback = out[3] XOR out[2]
- Shift left: {out[2:0], feedback}
- Seed: 4'b1001

```
module lfsr4 (
  input  logic clk, rst_n, en,
  output logic [3:0] out
);
```

Testbench must:
1. Run LFSR for 15 cycles (2^4-1 = 15 unique states)
2. Verify no state repeats (all 15 states unique)
3. Verify sequence starts: 1001 → 0011 → 0110 → 1101
4. Verify state never becomes 4'b0000 (illegal state)
5. Print each state

Module name: `tb_lfsr4`
hints: feedback = lfsr_reg[3] ^ lfsr_reg[2]
lfsr_reg <= {lfsr_reg[2:0], feedback}  // shift left, insert feedback at LSB
Seed must be non-zero: 4'b1001 gives maximal sequence
15 unique states for 4-bit LFSR (excludes all-zeros)
lab.solutionExplanation: A maximal-length LFSR visits 2^N-1 states before repeating. The polynomial x^4+x^3+1 gives feedback=bit[3] XOR bit[2]. Shifting left and inserting feedback at LSB gives the sequence. The all-zeros state is forbidden (feedback=0, stays at zero forever). Seed 1001 gives sequence: 1001→0011→0110→1101→1010→...

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your LBIST knowledge.

quiz.q: LFSR in LBIST is used as:
options: A pseudo-random pattern generator for test inputs
A short signature (fingerprint) for pass/fail comparison
Different faults produce the same signature — fault not detected

quiz.q: LBIST is preferred for automotive chips because:
options: It enables periodic self-test in the field without ATE

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: JTAG IEEE 1149.1 — Boundary Scan for board-level testing.
keyPoints: JTAG = Joint Test Action Group, now IEEE 1149.1 standard
4 mandatory pins: TCK, TMS, TDI, TDO (plus optional TRST)
TAP: Test Access Port — the JTAG controller state machine
Boundary scan: flip-flops at every I/O pin for board-level test
Instructions: BYPASS, SAMPLE/PRELOAD, EXTEST, IDCODE
BSDL: Boundary Scan Description Language — describes device
Used for: board-level interconnect test, chip programming, debug
subLevels.content: JTAG (IEEE 1149.1) defines a standard test interface used on virtually every modern chip. The TAP (Test Access Port) controller is a 16-state FSM controlled by TCK (test clock) and TMS (test mode select). TDI/TDO form a serial data path. Boundary scan cells are placed at every I/O pin — they allow the chip to drive/sense its own pins for board-level interconnect testing (finding open/short circuits on PCB traces between chips). The IDCODE instruction reads a 32-bit device identifier. BYPASS creates a 1-bit shift path through the chip. EXTEST drives the pins from boundary scan cells. JTAG is also used for FPGA programming and CPU debug (via JTAG-based debuggers).

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: TAP controller states and JTAG protocol sequences.
keyPoints: TAP states: Test-Logic-Reset, Run-Test/Idle, Shift-DR, Shift-IR...
TMS=1: moves toward reset; TMS=0: moves into active states
To enter Shift-DR: TMS sequence = 0,1,0,0 from Run-Test/Idle
IR length: typically 4-8 bits, defines which register to access
BYPASS register: 1-bit, always present, minimum path
IDCODE register: 32-bit, manufacturers' device identifier
Daisy-chain: multiple chips TDO→TDI for board scan

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace through a JTAG IDCODE read transaction step by step.
keyPoints: Step 1: Reset TAP — TMS=1 for 5 TCK cycles
Step 2: Go to Shift-IR — TMS=0,1,1,0,0
Step 3: Shift in IDCODE instruction (e.g., 4'b0001)
Step 4: Go to Shift-DR — TMS=1,1,0,0
Step 5: Shift 32 TCK cycles — read TDO each cycle
Step 6: Parse 32-bit IDCODE
IDCODE format: [31:28]=version, [27:12]=part, [11:1]=manufacturer, [0]=1

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a TAP controller FSM with BYPASS and IDCODE support.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 6: TAP Controller (Simplified)

Implement a simplified TAP controller with 6 key states:
```
module tap_ctrl (
  input  logic tck, tms, tdi, trst_n,
  output logic tdo,
  output logic shift_dr, shift_ir,
  output logic update_dr, update_ir,
  output logic capture_dr
);
```

States needed: TEST_RESET, RUN_IDLE, SELECT_DR, CAPTURE_DR, SHIFT_DR, UPDATE_DR

State transitions (TMS=0/1):
- TEST_RESET: TMS=0→RUN_IDLE, TMS=1→TEST_RESET
- RUN_IDLE: TMS=0→RUN_IDLE, TMS=1→SELECT_DR
- SELECT_DR: TMS=0→CAPTURE_DR, TMS=1→TEST_RESET
- CAPTURE_DR: TMS=0→SHIFT_DR, TMS=1→UPDATE_DR
- SHIFT_DR: TMS=0→SHIFT_DR, TMS=1→UPDATE_DR
- UPDATE_DR: TMS=0→RUN_IDLE, TMS=1→SELECT_DR

Assert output signals based on current state.
Write testbench to verify RESET→RUN_IDLE→SELECT_DR→CAPTURE_DR→SHIFT_DR sequence.
hints: State transitions follow TMS: 0→lower state, 1→higher/reset
shift_dr = (state == SHIFT_DR)
capture_dr = (state == CAPTURE_DR)
send_tms(0) from RUN_IDLE stays in RUN_IDLE
send_tms(1) from RUN_IDLE goes to SELECT_DR
lab.solutionExplanation: The TAP controller is a 16-state (simplified to 6 here) FSM clocked by TCK. TMS controls state transitions. SHIFT_DR is the state where test data is shifted in/out via TDI/TDO. The controller drives shift_dr, capture_dr, update_dr signals that control data registers (BYPASS, IDCODE, boundary scan). This exact FSM is in every JTAG-compliant chip.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your JTAG knowledge.

options: Creates a 1-bit shift path through the chip to minimize chain length
At every I/O pin of the chip to control/observe pin states

quiz.q: TMS=1 for 5+ TCK cycles always causes:
options: Test-Logic-Reset — the TAP controller always resets

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Scan compression — reducing test time while maintaining fault coverage.
keyPoints: Scan compression reduces test time by 10x-100x without losing coverage
Decompressor: expands a small number of ATE channels to many scan chains
Compactor: compresses many chain outputs back to few ATE channels
X-tolerance: handles don't-care (X) values from multiple chains
EDT: Embedded Deterministic Test — Mentor's compression technology
DFTMAX: Synopsys compression technology
Compression ratio: internal_chains / external_channels
subLevels.content: Modern chips have millions of flip-flops requiring thousands of scan chains. ATE has limited channels and tester memory. Scan compression solves this: a decompressor (XOR network) expands a small ATE pattern into a much larger internal pattern that fills all chains simultaneously. The compactor (another XOR network) compresses all chain responses back to the few ATE channels. The key enabler is don't-care (X) values — ATPG patterns typically specify only 1-5% of bits; the remaining 95%+ are X. The decompressor fills X positions with pseudo-random values, allowing multiple chains to be tested simultaneously with deterministic patterns for the critical bits.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: XOR decompressor and spatial compactor design patterns.
keyPoints: Decompressor: XOR tree expanding N inputs to M outputs (M >> N)
Each output = XOR of a subset of inputs (defined by matrix)
Compactor: XOR tree combining M outputs into N outputs
Compression ratio = M/N (internal/external channels)
EDT ratio: typically 8:1 to 64:1
X-filling: ATPG assigns X values to avoid conflicts in decompressor
Phase shifter: ensures different scan chains get different patterns

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace a 4:1 compression scenario — shift, capture, compact.
keyPoints: 4 internal chains, 1 external channel → 4:1 compression
Decompressor generates different patterns for each chain from 1 input
4 chains shift simultaneously — test time = longest chain length
4 chain responses XOR-compressed to 1 channel
ATE reads 1/4 the data of uncompressed scan
Compression ratio 4:1 = 4x faster test or 4x less ATE memory

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a 2-to-4 XOR decompressor and 4-to-2 compactor.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 7: Scan Compression — Decompressor and Compactor

Implement a 2:1 compression system:

**Decompressor** (2 inputs → 4 outputs):
- chain[0] = in[0] ^ in[1]
- chain[1] = in[0]
- chain[2] = in[1]
- chain[3] = in[0] ^ in[1]

**Compactor** (4 inputs → 2 outputs):
- out[0] = chain[0] ^ chain[2]
- out[1] = chain[1] ^ chain[3]

Testbench must:
1. Apply all 4 input combinations (00,01,10,11)
2. Print decompressor outputs for each
3. Feed decompressor output directly to compactor
4. Verify compactor doesn't lose fault information:
   - Apply in=2'b01 → decompressor→compactor → check out
   - Apply in=2'b10 → check different out
5. Verify in=2'b00 gives out=2'b00 (no fault = no signal)

Module: `tb_compression`
hints: assign ext_out[0] = chain_out[0] ^ chain_out[2];
assign ext_out[1] = chain_out[1] ^ chain_out[3];
For ext_in=2'b00: all chain_in=0, all chain_out=0, ext_out=0
lab.solutionExplanation: The decompressor expands 2 ATE channels to 4 internal chains using XOR combinations. The compactor compresses 4 chain responses back to 2 ATE channels. Zero input → zero output ensures no false failures. Non-zero (fault) input propagates through — any fault in any chain creates non-zero output. This is the core of Mentor EDT and Synopsys DFTMAX compression architectures.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your scan compression knowledge.

options: 32 internal chains driven by 1 external ATE channel

quiz.q: Don't-care (X) values in ATPG enable compression because:
options: X values are faster to test
X bits can be filled by decompressor without creating conflicts
X means the bit is untestable
Expands few ATE channels to many internal scan chains
Embedded Deterministic Test — Mentor's compression technology

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: At-speed testing — detecting timing defects at functional frequency.
keyPoints: At-speed testing runs capture at full functional clock speed
Detects: resistive opens, resistive shorts, crosstalk-induced delays
Launch-on-shift (LOS): last shift clock launches the capture
Launch-on-capture (LOC): dedicated launch cycle before capture
Transition fault: gate cannot make 0→1 or 1→0 within one cycle
Skewed-load technique: allows at-speed capture in scan chains
LBIST at-speed: runs BIST patterns at functional frequency
subLevels.content: Static stuck-at ATPG finds permanent defects (opens, shorts). But some defects cause timing failures — a path is slightly too slow and fails only at full speed. At-speed testing applies patterns and captures at the functional clock frequency. Launch-on-Shift (LOS): the last shift clock edge launches a transition at the flip-flop output, and the next (functional-speed) clock captures the result. Launch-on-Capture (LOC): two full-speed clocks are applied — the first launches, the second captures. LOC is more controllable but requires the circuit to be set up before both clocks. At-speed testing adds 20-30% test time but catches yield-killing timing defects invisible to stuck-at ATPG.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: At-speed test control structures and timing patterns.
keyPoints: LOS: last shift clock = first (slow) launch clock
LOC: separate functional-speed launch clock after shift
Two-cycle capture: apply L1 (launch), L2 (capture) at speed
Clock controller: generates slow scan clock and fast functional clock
ATE timing: must generate two closely-spaced capture clocks
ATPG option: -transition for transition fault targeting
Path tracing: identify critical paths for at-speed test focus

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Trace a Launch-on-Capture at-speed test sequence.
keyPoints: Step 1: Shift in initializing pattern (slow clock, SE=1)
Step 2: Launch clock pulse (fast, SE=0) — launches transition
Step 3: Capture clock pulse (fast, SE=0) — captures at speed
Step 4: Shift out captured data (slow clock, SE=1)
Step 5: Compare to expected — mismatch = transition fault
Defect: a slow path misses capture window = FAIL detected

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a clock controller for at-speed LOC test mode.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 8: At-Speed Clock Controller

Implement a clock controller that generates the LOC at-speed test sequence:

```
module atspeed_ctrl (
  input  logic clk_fast,    // functional speed clock
  input  logic clk_slow,    // scan/ATE clock
  input  logic scan_en,     // 1=shift, 0=capture
  input  logic test_mode,   // 1=test, 0=functional
  input  logic loc_en,      // 1=LOC capture mode
  output logic clk_dff,     // clock to all flip-flops
  output logic capture_done // signals capture complete
);
```

Behavior:
- `test_mode=0`: clk_dff = clk_fast (normal operation)
- `test_mode=1, scan_en=1`: clk_dff = clk_slow (scan shift)
- `test_mode=1, scan_en=0, loc_en=0`: clk_dff = 0 (hold)
- `test_mode=1, scan_en=0, loc_en=1`: allow 2 clk_fast pulses then assert capture_done

Write testbench to verify all 4 modes switch correctly.
hints: Functional: clk_dff = clk_fast
Scan shift: clk_dff = clk_slow when scan_en=1
LOC: allow clk_fast to pass, count 2 pulses then assert capture_done
Always_comb for clock mux ensures no glitches
lab.solutionExplanation: The clock controller muxes between functional clock, scan clock, and gated states based on test mode signals. During LOC, exactly 2 fast clock pulses are generated — the first launches the transition (functional state), the second captures the result. capture_done tells the system when to switch back to scan shift mode. This is the core of at-speed DFT infrastructure.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your at-speed testing knowledge.

options: Stuck-at ATPG is too slow to run
Some defects only manifest as timing failures at functional frequency
A dedicated launch clock pulse before capture at functional speed
Gates that can transition but may be too slow at speed

quiz.q: Why are two clock pulses needed in LOC?
options: First pulse launches transition, second pulse captures result at speed

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Hierarchical DFT — testing complex SoCs block by block.
keyPoints: Large SoCs have billions of transistors — cannot test as one flat design
Hierarchical DFT: test each IP block independently, then assemble
Core wrapper: isolates block for standalone test and chip-level test
IEEE 1500: standard for embedded core testing (wrapping standard)
Test access mechanism (TAM): routes test data to/from each core
Modular test: each block has its own ATPG patterns
Top-level test: tests interconnects between blocks
subLevels.content: Modern SoCs contain hundreds of IP blocks from multiple vendors. Hierarchical DFT wraps each block with a standardized test interface (IEEE 1500 core wrapper) that allows the block to be: (1) tested in isolation before integration, (2) tested as part of the assembled chip using the same test patterns. The core wrapper adds bypass logic, wrapper cells at block boundaries, and a test interface. A Test Access Mechanism (TAM) distributes test data from chip-level JTAG to each block's wrapper. This enables IP reuse with pre-verified test patterns — the block's ATPG patterns work whether the block is tested standalone or embedded in the SoC.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: IEEE 1500 core wrapper cell and TAM interface patterns.
keyPoints: Wrapper cell: at each block I/O, captures/drives boundary values
WI (Wrapper Input): parallel→serial conversion of block inputs
WO (Wrapper Output): captures block output for shift-out
WSI/WSO: wrapper serial in/out — connects to TAM
WBY: wrapper bypass — skip this block in test chain
SELECTWIR: selects wrapper instruction register
TAM: typically a JTAG-based distribution network to each block

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read a hierarchical DFT architecture for a 3-block SoC.
keyPoints: SoC: CPU block + Memory Controller + USB block
Each block wrapped with IEEE 1500 wrapper
JTAG TAP at chip level distributes test access
TAM chains: CPU wrapper → Mem wrapper → USB wrapper
Block test: isolate one block, apply its ATPG patterns
Interconnect test: use boundary scan to test chip-level wiring
Total test time = sum of all block test times (can parallelize)

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Implement a simple core wrapper for a 2-input, 1-output block.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 9: IEEE 1500 Core Wrapper (Simplified)

Implement a wrapper for a 2-input AND gate block:

```
// Core: out = a & b
module core_and (input a, b, output out);
  assign out = a & b;
endmodule
```

Wrapper must:
1. Have wrapper cells on inputs a,b and output out
2. In functional mode (sel=0): a,b come from chip pins; out goes to chip
3. In test mode (sel=1, cap=1): capture chip_a, chip_b into wrapper regs
4. In test mode (sel=1, shi=1): shift data in from wsi, out through wso
5. In test mode, drive core inputs from wrapper cells (test values)

Test the wrapper by:
- Loading test vector a=1,b=1 via wsi shift
- Switching to capture mode — verify core out=1
- Shifting out captured output — verify wso sequence

Module: `tb_core_wrapper`
hints: wrapper_cell captures 'd' when cap=1, shifts 'wsi' when shi=1
Chain: wsi→wa→wb→wo→wso (serial chain through all boundary cells)
sel=1: core sees wrapper register values instead of chip pins
sel=0: core sees chip pins directly (functional mode)
lab.solutionExplanation: The core wrapper isolates the block for testing. Wrapper cells at each boundary capture/shift boundary values. The serial chain wsi→wa→wb→wo→wso allows ATPG patterns to be shifted in and responses shifted out. sel=1 breaks the chip-level connections and substitutes wrapper register values — the block is now isolated and testable independently.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your hierarchical DFT knowledge.

options: Standard for embedded core wrapping and test access

quiz.q: A core wrapper isolates a block by:
options: Using wrapper cells to intercept boundary signals in test mode

quiz.q: TAM (Test Access Mechanism) is used to:
options: Route test data from chip-level JTAG to each embedded block

quiz.q: Interconnect test in hierarchical DFT tests:
options: Wiring between blocks at the chip level

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Industry DFT flows — complete chip DFT from specification to silicon validation.
keyPoints: DFT specification: target coverage, test time, chip test pins
DFT insertion: scan, MBIST, JTAG added in RTL or post-synthesis
Formal DFT check: verify scan chain connectivity is correct
ATPG run: TetraMAX or Encounter Test generates patterns
Pattern simulation: verify patterns detect target faults
Tester translation: convert STIL to ATE format (93000, V93000, Titan)
Silicon bring-up: first silicon validation using DFT infrastructure
subLevels.content: The complete industry DFT flow starts at specification: what test coverage is required (99% stuck-at, 95% transition), how many test pins are available, what test time budget exists. DFT engineers then insert scan, MBIST, and JTAG during RTL coding or post-synthesis. Formal verification checks that scan chains are correctly connected (no broken chains). ATPG generates patterns targeting 99%+ coverage. Patterns are simulated at gate level to confirm expected coverage. Final patterns are translated to the ATE format. In silicon bring-up, DFT is the first thing used — JTAG to read IDCODE (is the chip alive?), scan to check register functionality, MBIST to qualify memories.

subLevels.title: Syntax & Patterns
subLevels.summary: Industry DFT scripts — TetraMAX ATPG and Encounter Test commands.
keyPoints: TetraMAX: Synopsys ATPG tool — most widely used in industry
Encounter Test: Cadence ATPG tool
read_netlist: load gate-level netlist into ATPG tool
add_dft_signals: define scan, test_mode, JTAG pins
run_atpg: generate test patterns for target coverage
report_statistics: shows fault coverage, pattern count
write_patterns: export to STIL for ATE
subLevels.content:
# TetraMAX ATPG Flow (industry commands)

# Step 1: Read design
tmax> read_netlist design_scan.v -design my_chip
tmax> read_netlist tech_lib.v

# Step 2: Define DFT signals
tmax> add_dft_signals -scan_enable scan_en
tmax> add_dft_signals -test_clock clk -period 10
tmax> set_dft_signal -type ScanDataIn  -port tdi
tmax> set_dft_signal -type ScanDataOut -port tdo

# Step 3: Build model
tmax> build_model -verbose
tmax> check_design -count

# Step 4: Run ATPG
tmax> run_atpg -effort high
# → generates patterns targeting stuck-at faults

# Step 5: Report coverage
tmax> report_statistics
# Stuck-at coverage: 99.3%
# Patterns: 1,847

# Step 6: Add transition fault patterns
tmax> set_fault_type transition
tmax> run_atpg -effort high
tmax> report_statistics

# Step 7: Write patterns
tmax> write_patterns ./output/chip_test.stil -format stil
tmax> write_patterns ./output/chip_test.wgl  -format wgl

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read a complete ATPG statistics report from a real chip run.
keyPoints: Total faults = 2× internal nets (SA0 and SA1 each)
Detected faults = covered by at least 1 test pattern
Possibly detected = may be detected (simulation X-states)
Undetectable = logically redundant (cannot be detected)
Target: (detected + possibly detected) / (total - undetectable) > 99%
Pattern count trade-off: more patterns = higher coverage but longer test
subLevels.reportContent:
# TetraMAX ATPG Statistics Report

================================================

  Detected:              1,268,104   (98.71%)

  Undetectable (redun):      4,218   ( 0.33%)
  Not detected:              2,121   ( 0.17%)
================================================
  Fault Coverage:
  Stuck-at coverage:  99.51%  PASS (target: 99%)
  (detected + possibly) / (total - undetectable)
================================================
  Pattern Statistics:
  Total patterns:         2,847
  Shift length (max):       384 cycles
  Estimated test time:     12.4 ms at 100MHz
================================================

  [INFO] 67 faults untestable (async reset paths)
  [WARN] 15 chains with imbalanced lengths
================================================

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: Write a complete DFT testbench simulating ATE test execution.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 10: ATE Test Simulation

Simulate an ATE test execution for a scan-based chip:

```
// Chip with 8-flop scan chain and combinational logic
module dut_chip (
  input  logic clk, rst_n,
  input  logic scan_en, tdi,
  input  logic [3:0] pi,    // primary inputs
  output logic [1:0] po,    // primary outputs
  output logic tdo
);
```

Write an ATE simulation testbench that:
1. Resets chip and verifies TDO=0
2. **ATPG Pattern 1** — shift in and capture:
   - Shift in: 8'b10110100 (MSB first through TDI)
   - Set primary inputs: pi=4'b1010
   - Capture (SE=0, 1 clock)
   - Shift out 8 bits and print TDO sequence
3. **ATPG Pattern 2** — second pattern:
   - Shift in: 8'b01001011
   - pi=4'b0101
   - Capture and shift out
4. Print PASS/FAIL for each pattern
5. Report: total patterns, pass count

Module: `tb_ate_sim`
hints: Shift in: tdi gets shift_in[7] first (MSB), then [6], ..., [0]
Shift out: read tdo after each clock during shift-out phase
expected_out can be 'x' (don't care) — use 'bxxxxxxxx for initial runs
Real ATE compares shifted_out to expected from ATPG simulation
lab.solutionExplanation: The ATE simulation applies exactly what physical ATE does: shift in → capture → shift out → compare. The shift-in loads the ATPG-determined scan state. Capture applies one functional clock to observe circuit response. Shift-out reads the response for comparison. Real ATE runs this at MHz speeds with thousands of patterns. This lab models the fundamental ATE test loop.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Test your industry DFT knowledge.

quiz.q: The first thing tested on new silicon using DFT is usually:
options: JTAG IDCODE read — confirms chip is powered and accessible
Fault coverage percentage, pattern count, and test time estimate
Faults that will cause chip failure
Logically redundant faults — circuit structure makes them untestable
Transfer test patterns from ATPG tool to ATE for silicon test

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: What DFT interviews at top companies actually test.
keyPoints: Explain stuck-at fault model and why it matters — always asked
Draw a scan chain and explain shift/capture/shift-out — always asked
Write a scan flop RTL — coding round in every interview
Explain MBIST architecture — block diagram question
JTAG TAP FSM — draw the state machine
Coverage calculation — given numbers, compute fault coverage
Companies: Qualcomm, Intel, MediaTek, Apple, Samsung, TSMC
subLevels.content: DFT interviews test both concept depth and RTL coding ability. You must be able to explain every concept from first principles — not just memorize definitions. The most common coding question: write a scan flop in RTL. You must code it correctly with scan MUX, active-low reset, and proper SO connection. The most common concept question: explain the complete scan test flow (shift in → capture → shift out). Know: why is capture done in functional mode? What causes X propagation in scan? What is fault coverage and how is it calculated? Industry numbers: 99% stuck-at, 95% transition are common targets.

subLevels.title: Common Interview Patterns
subLevels.summary: Top 10 DFT coding and concept patterns asked in every interview.
keyPoints: 1. Scan flip-flop with scan MUX — RTL coding
2. 4-flop scan chain with TDI/TDO — connection coding
3. Clock gating with scan bypass (ICG) — RTL coding
4. March C- algorithm — write the pseudo-code
5. LFSR 4-bit maximal — RTL coding
6. TAP controller states — draw/explain FSM
7. ATPG: find test vector for SA0 — calculation
8. Fault coverage formula — given numbers, compute
9. Scan compression: decompressor XOR network
10. LOC at-speed sequence — explain step by step

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Solve 5 classic DFT interview questions.
keyPoints: Q: Why is scan enable high during shift but low during capture?
Q: What is an untestable fault? Give an example
Q: Why can't we test SRAMs with scan chains?
Q: What causes X propagation in scan and how do you fix it?
Q: What is the difference between LBIST and MBIST?

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: The classic DFT interview challenge — complete scan chain testbench.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 11: Classic DFT Interview — Full Scan Test

Write a complete scan test for a 4-bit register with XOR reduction:

```
module dut_4bit (
  input  logic clk, rst_n,
  input  logic [3:0] d,
  input  logic scan_en, tdi,
  output logic [3:0] q,
  output logic parity,   // q[3]^q[2]^q[1]^q[0]
  output logic tdo
);
```

**Must include:**
1. Scan flop for each of the 4 bits (chain: tdi→ff0→ff1→ff2→ff3→tdo)
2. Parity output = XOR of all Q bits
3. Full testbench that:
   - Resets all flops
   - Shifts in `1010` 
   - Verifies parity=0 after capture (1^0^1^0=0)
   - Shifts in `1111`
   - Verifies parity=0 (1^1^1^1=0)
   - Shifts in `1110`
   - Verifies parity=1 (1^1^1^0=1)
   - Reports PASS/FAIL

Module: `tb_dft_interview`
hints: Pattern shifts LSB first: pattern[0]→ff0, pattern[1]→ff1, ...
So for q=1010: shift_in(4'b0101) — ff0 gets bit0=1, ff1 gets bit1=0...
assign parity = ^q;  // SystemVerilog reduction XOR
Capture: SE=0, one clock — functional data (d) captured
lab.solutionExplanation: This is the complete DFT interview answer. The scan chain connects ff0→ff1→ff2→ff3 with tdi→so→si connections. Shift loads values into chain. Capture evaluates functional logic (parity XOR in this case). The parity check verifies the chain values were correctly loaded and the combinational logic (XOR reduction) works correctly. This exact flow — shift in, capture, check output — is how all silicon is tested.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: DFT interview-style questions.

quiz.q: Most common DFT interview coding question:
options: Write a scan flip-flop with scan MUX and active-low reset

quiz.q: During scan shift (SE=1) the flip-flop captures:
options: Scan input SI from previous flop

quiz.q: X propagation in scan is most commonly caused by:
options: Uninitialized flops or uncontrolled async resets

subLevels.title: Concept
subLevels.summary: Complete DFT architecture for a real SoC — the final boss.
keyPoints: SoC DFT plan: scan + MBIST + JTAG + at-speed + compression all together
DFT budget: area overhead, pin count, test time, coverage targets
Multi-clock domain: each domain needs own scan clock and control
Codec architecture: decompressor→scan chains→compactor in one block
BIST hierarchy: chip-level BIST controller manages all sub-BIST blocks
Safety DFT: ISO 26262 requires LBIST for periodic field testing
Tapeout DFT checklist: coverage, chains, MBIST, JTAG all verified
subLevels.content: A complete SoC DFT architecture integrates all test methods. Scan compression (DFTMAX/EDT) provides 50:1 compression for the thousands of internal scan chains. MBIST covers all SRAMs and ROMs. JTAG provides the top-level test access. LBIST enables periodic self-test for safety applications. The DFT engineer must balance: test coverage (higher = better quality but more patterns), test time (longer = more cost on ATE), area overhead (more DFT logic = more chip area), and pin count (limited test pins available). Final DFT signoff requires: ≥99% stuck-at coverage, ≥95% transition coverage, 0 broken scan chains, all memories with MBIST, JTAG functional.

subLevels.title: Architecture Overview
subLevels.summary: Complete SoC DFT architecture — all components and connections.
keyPoints: Scan: 256 internal chains compressed to 8 external — 32:1 ratio
MBIST: controller tests 12 SRAM blocks sequentially
JTAG: TAP → selects scan, MBIST, IDCODE, BYPASS
At-speed: LOC for transition faults on critical paths
LBIST: LFSR+MISR for automotive safety periodic test
Test pins: 8 scan data + 4 JTAG = 12 test pins total
Compression: input XOR decompressor + output MISR compactor

subLevels.title: Walkthrough
subLevels.summary: Read a complete DFT signoff checklist for a real SoC.
keyPoints: All scan chains must be verified by formal check
MBIST must pass on all memories before scan patterns
Stuck-at coverage ≥ 99% mandatory before tapeout
Transition coverage ≥ 95% for performance silicon
JTAG IDCODE must be readable after first power-on
X-bounding: all undefined states must be set before capture
Final pattern count and test time must fit ATE budget

subLevels.title: Lab
subLevels.summary: FINAL BOSS — Complete DFT system: scan + MBIST + JTAG in one design.

lab.instructions:
# Lab 12: FINAL BOSS — Complete DFT System

Integrate all DFT components for a small processor:

```
module soc_dft (
  // Functional
  input  logic clk, rst_n,
  input  logic [3:0] instr,
  output logic [3:0] result,
  // JTAG
  input  logic tck, tms, tdi_jtag, trst_n,
  output logic tdo_jtag,
  // Scan
  input  logic scan_en, tdi_scan,
  output logic tdo_scan,
  // BIST
  input  logic bist_en,
  output logic bist_done, bist_pass
);
```

Integrate:
1. **4-flop scan chain** on the processor register (tdi_scan→ff0→ff1→ff2→ff3→tdo_scan)
2. **Simplified TAP** (TEST_RESET, RUN_IDLE, SHIFT_DR): routes tdi_jtag to either scan chain or IDCODE based on instruction
3. **IDCODE register** (32-bit): hardcode value 32'hDEAD_BEEF
4. **MBIST block** (4-cell memory): run march M0+M1+M2 when bist_en=1
5. **Testbench** that:
   - Reads IDCODE via JTAG
   - Runs MBIST — verify pass
   - Applies one scan pattern via tdi_scan
   - Prints full test report

Module: `tb_soc_dft_final`
hints: JTAG IDCODE: shift 32 bits out via tdo_jtag with TMS=0
MBIST: assert bist_en for 1 cycle, wait for bist_done
Scan: shift in LSB first through tdi_scan, capture with SE=0
result=q captures the scan chain contents after capture clock
lab.solutionExplanation: The final boss integrates all DFT disciplines in one design: scan chain for logic testing, JTAG for test access and IDCODE, MBIST for memory testing. The testbench runs exactly the sequence used in real silicon bring-up: IDCODE first (chip alive?), MBIST second (memories good?), scan patterns last (logic good?). This is the complete DFT architect skill set — what you need to design and verify DFT for production silicon at Intel, Qualcomm, MediaTek, or Apple.

subLevels.title: Quiz
subLevels.summary: Final boss — complete DFT architect knowledge.

quiz.q: The first DFT test run on new silicon is typically:
options: JTAG IDCODE read — verifies the chip is powered and the TAP is functional
Periodic in-field self-test (LBIST) to detect field failures during operation
